questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Sediment Core
When Dr. Lena Ortiz pushed a narrow tube into the marsh mud, the students watching from the boardwalk seemed disappointed. They had expected nets, boats, or dramatic wildlife. Instead, Ortiz lifted a dark cylinder of wet sediment from the tube and placed it carefully into a labeled tray.
“This is a timeline,” she said.
A sediment core forms as layers of soil, plant material, pollen, ash, and tiny shells settle over time. The newest material is usually near the top, while older layers are deeper. By studying these layers, scientists can learn how a marsh has changed. Some layers may show past floods. Others may show periods when saltwater moved farther inland. Pollen can suggest what plants once grew nearby.
Ortiz’s team was studying whether the marsh had been keeping pace with rising water. A healthy marsh can sometimes build upward as plants trap sediment and leave behind roots. But if water rises faster than the marsh can grow, plants may die and open water may replace habitat. The core helped the team compare recent sediment growth with older patterns.
The students later examined a sliced section of the core in the lab. A thin pale band showed where a storm had pushed sand across the marsh years earlier. Darker layers contained plant roots, suggesting stronger marsh growth. The core was not exciting in the usual sense, but it held evidence that could not be seen from the boardwalk.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A sediment core can help scientists study how a marsh has changed over time.
choices.B: Scientists use sediment cores only to collect dramatic wildlife.
choices.C: The marsh study proved that rising water has no effect on plants.
choices.D: Students learned that marsh mud contains no useful information.

questions.correctAnswerText: A sediment core can help scientists study how a marsh has changed over time.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that sediment layers contain evidence of past storms, plant growth, and marsh change over time.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Sediment Core.”
Why does Dr. Ortiz call the sediment core “a timeline”?

choices.A: Its layers can show changes that occurred over time.
choices.B: It lists the students’ class schedule.
choices.C: It records exact conversations on the boardwalk.
choices.D: It shows only the current water temperature.

questions.correctAnswerText: Its layers can show changes that occurred over time.
questions.explanation: A sediment core is like a timeline because newer and older layers preserve evidence of different periods.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Sediment Core.”
Which TWO types of evidence can be found in a sediment core?

choices.A: Pollen from plants that once grew nearby
choices.B: Sand layers from past storms
choices.C: Printed maps of the boardwalk
choices.D: Wildlife photographs taken by students

questions.correctAnswerText: Pollen from plants that once grew nearby | Sand layers from past storms
questions.explanation: The passage says pollen and storm-deposited sand can appear in sediment layers.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Sediment Core.”
What can be inferred from the statement that a healthy marsh can sometimes “build upward”?

choices.A: Some marshes can add material fast enough to respond to changing water levels.
choices.B: Marshes rise because students lift them with tubes.
choices.C: Marsh plants grow only when there is no sediment.
choices.D: A marsh becomes healthier when plant roots disappear.

questions.correctAnswerText: Some marshes can add material fast enough to respond to changing water levels.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that plants can trap sediment and leave roots behind, helping marshes build upward.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Sediment Core.”
In the sentence “The core helped the team compare recent sediment growth with older patterns,” patterns most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=repeated conditions or trends
questions.explanation: In context, patterns means conditions or trends that appear in the sediment record.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Sediment Core.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Newer material is usually near the top, while older layers are deeper.
tiles.B: A thin pale band showed where a storm had pushed sand across the marsh.
tiles.D: The students used the core to measure parking spaces.

targets.T1: Evidence that a core records time order
targets.T2: Evidence of a past storm event

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Newer material is usually near the top, while older layers are deeper.; T2=A thin pale band showed where a storm had pushed sand across the marsh.; T3=Darker layers contained plant roots.
questions.explanation: Each tile provides evidence for the matching target.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Travel Expense Reimbursement Procedure
To: Sales, Training, and Field Service Employees
From: Finance Department
Subject: Travel Expense Reimbursement Procedure
Beginning June 2, all travel expense requests must be submitted within ten business days after the trip ends. This procedure applies to mileage, hotel stays, meals, parking, tolls, baggage fees, and approved ride services.
Employees must attach receipts for expenses over $15. For mileage, employees should list the starting location, destination, business purpose, and total miles driven. Do not combine personal travel with business mileage. If a trip includes both personal and business stops, record only the business portion and explain it briefly in the notes section.
Meal reimbursements must follow the daily limit listed in the employee handbook. Alcohol, entertainment, traffic fines, and personal purchases are not reimbursable. If a receipt includes both reimbursable and non-reimbursable items, circle the business expense and write the adjusted amount clearly.
Incomplete requests will be returned for correction. The finance department reviews requests every Tuesday and Friday. Submitting accurate information helps reimbursements move faster and reduces follow-up emails.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how employees should submit travel expenses for reimbursement
choices.B: To announce that employees may combine personal travel with business mileage
choices.C: To require finance staff to reimburse all purchases without review
choices.D: To cancel mileage reimbursement for all employees

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how employees should submit travel expenses for reimbursement
questions.explanation: The document explains what expenses are covered, what records are needed, and how to submit a request.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Travel Expense Reimbursement Procedure.”
When must travel expense requests be submitted?

choices.A: Within ten business days after the trip ends
choices.B: Before the employee is assigned any trip
choices.C: Only at the end of the calendar year
choices.D: After the finance department sends three reminders

questions.correctAnswerText: Within ten business days after the trip ends
questions.explanation: The document states that requests must be submitted within ten business days after a trip ends.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Travel Expense Reimbursement Procedure.”
Which TWO items are not reimbursable?

choices.A: Alcohol
choices.B: Traffic fines
choices.C: Approved business mileage
choices.D: Parking for a business meeting

questions.correctAnswerText: Alcohol | Traffic fines
questions.explanation: The procedure states that alcohol, entertainment, traffic fines, and personal purchases are not reimbursable.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Travel Expense Reimbursement Procedure.”
What should an employee do if a receipt includes both business and personal items?

choices.A: Circle the business expense and write the adjusted amount clearly.
choices.B: Submit the entire receipt without explanation.
choices.C: Remove the receipt from the request.
choices.D: Ask finance to guess which items were business expenses.

questions.correctAnswerText: Circle the business expense and write the adjusted amount clearly.
questions.explanation: This is the required action when a receipt includes both reimbursable and non-reimbursable items.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Travel Expense Reimbursement Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains why accurate requests matter?

choices.A: “Beginning June 2, all travel expense requests must be submitted within ten business days after the trip ends.”
choices.B: “This procedure applies to mileage, hotel stays, meals, parking, tolls, baggage fees, and approved ride services.”
choices.C: “Submitting accurate information helps reimbursements move faster and reduces follow-up emails.”
choices.D: “The finance department reviews requests every Tuesday and Friday.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Submitting accurate information helps reimbursements move faster and reduces follow-up emails.”
questions.explanation: This sentence explains why accurate expense requests matter.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Use Plain-Language Insurance Summaries
Health insurance companies should provide plain-language summaries after they process medical claims. Many customers receive an explanation of benefits filled with terms such as deductible, coinsurance, allowed amount, adjustment, and out-of-pocket maximum. These terms may be accurate, but they can be difficult to understand when a person is trying to figure out what has been paid and what is still owed.
A plain-language summary would not replace the official document. It would appear at the beginning and explain the claim in ordinary wording: what service was billed, what the insurer paid, what the customer may owe, and what action, if any, the customer should take. This would help people spot possible billing errors and avoid confusing an explanation of benefits with an actual bill.
Clearer summaries could also reduce customer service calls. When customers cannot understand a document, they often call the insurer, the clinic, or both. A summary that explains the main points could save time for customers and staff.
Some insurers may argue that medical billing is too complex to simplify. It is true that details matter, and official documents must remain precise. But precision does not require confusion. A short summary can guide readers to the key information while leaving full details available for review.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Insurance companies should add plain-language summaries to help customers understand processed claims.
choices.B: Insurance companies should remove all official claim documents.
choices.C: Customers should ignore medical bills if the wording is difficult.
choices.D: Medical billing should be explained only through phone calls.

questions.correctAnswerText: Insurance companies should add plain-language summaries to help customers understand processed claims.
questions.explanation: The author argues that plain-language summaries would help customers understand what was paid and what they may owe.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Use Plain-Language Insurance Summaries.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that summaries could reduce confusion?

choices.A: Current documents often include terms such as deductible, coinsurance, and allowed amount.
choices.B: Official documents must remain precise.
choices.C: Insurance companies process medical claims.
choices.D: Some customers call clinics for appointment times.

questions.correctAnswerText: Current documents often include terms such as deductible, coinsurance, and allowed amount.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports the claim that customers may struggle with technical wording.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Use Plain-Language Insurance Summaries.”
Which TWO pieces of information should the plain-language summary include?

choices.A: What the insurer paid
choices.B: What the customer may owe
choices.C: The customer’s favorite clinic
choices.D: The doctor’s personal schedule

questions.correctAnswerText: What the insurer paid | What the customer may owe
questions.explanation: The passage says the summary should explain what the insurer paid and what the customer may owe.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Use Plain-Language Insurance Summaries.”
How does the author respond to the idea that medical billing is too complex to simplify?

choices.A: By arguing that a short summary can guide readers while full details remain available
choices.B: By saying official documents should no longer be accurate
choices.C: By claiming that customers never need billing details
choices.D: By suggesting that insurers stop processing claims

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that a short summary can guide readers while full details remain available
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges complexity but argues that a summary can coexist with precise official documents.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Use Plain-Language Insurance Summaries.”
Choose the sentence that would best strengthen the author’s argument if added after paragraph 3: [D1]

D1: In a customer survey, many callers said they contacted support because they could not tell whether the document was a bill.
The insurance company’s logo appears in the upper left corner of the document.
Some claim forms are printed on white paper.

questions.correct: D1=In a customer survey, many callers said they contacted support because they could not tell whether the document was a bill.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In a customer survey, many callers said they contacted support because they could not tell whether the document was a bill.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the claim that confusing documents increase customer service calls.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Use Plain-Language Insurance Summaries.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Customers can make better decisions when key claim information is explained clearly.
choices.B: Official insurance documents should contain no detailed information.
choices.C: All medical claims are simple enough to fit in one sentence.
choices.D: Customer service calls are never related to document confusion.

questions.correctAnswerText: Customers can make better decisions when key claim information is explained clearly.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that clearer information helps customers understand and act.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Last Row
Marisol had planted lettuce for six seasons, but always by someone else’s plan. Her father marked the rows, her aunt chose the seed packets, and Marisol followed behind with a trowel. This spring, her father handed her the notebook where the family recorded planting dates, weather, and harvest notes.
“You choose the last row,” he said.
The last row was narrow and uneven because the fence curved near the old pear tree. It was not the best place in the garden, but it was hers to decide. Marisol turned the notebook pages slowly. Last year, spinach had bolted early in that row. The year before, radishes had grown well because they were ready before the shade became too heavy.
She wanted to choose something impressive, something that would make her father nod before the first leaves appeared. Instead, she wrote “radishes” in the notebook and added the date.
For two weeks, the row looked like nothing. Then small green loops pushed through the soil. Marisol thinned the seedlings, watered carefully, and pulled weeds before they crowded the roots. When she finally lifted the first radish, it was round, red, and smaller than the pictures on the seed packet.
Her father rinsed it under the outdoor faucet and cut it in half. “Good choice for that row,” he said.
Marisol looked at the curved fence, the pear tree shade, and the notebook open on the bench. The garden suddenly seemed less like a place she helped in and more like a place she could learn to read.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Marisol must choose and care for a garden row using her own judgment.
choices.B: Marisol wants to remove the pear tree before planting.
choices.C: Marisol argues with her father about closing the garden.
choices.D: Marisol refuses to write anything in the family notebook.

questions.correctAnswerText: Marisol must choose and care for a garden row using her own judgment.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Marisol’s first responsibility for choosing and tending a row herself.

choices.B: Her aunt tells her that radishes are the only allowed crop.
choices.C: The seed packet says radishes never need sunlight.
choices.D: Her father marks the row and writes the choice for her.

questions.correctAnswerText: Past notes show radishes grew well there before the shade became heavy.
questions.explanation: Marisol uses the notebook’s past record to choose radishes for the difficult row.

choices.B: A book of unrelated stories about the pear tree
choices.C: A rule that prevents Marisol from making any choice
choices.D: A list of vegetables that failed in every row

questions.correctAnswerText: A record of experience that helps Marisol make a thoughtful decision
questions.explanation: The notebook contains planting dates, weather, and harvest notes that guide Marisol’s choice.

choices.A: She thins the seedlings.
choices.B: She pulls weeds before they crowd the roots.
choices.C: She lets the row dry out for two weeks.
choices.D: She closes the notebook without reading it.
choices.E: She removes the fence near the pear tree.

questions.correctAnswerText: She thins the seedlings. | She pulls weeds before they crowd the roots.
questions.explanation: These actions show Marisol taking care of the row after planting.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Last Row.”
What does the final sentence suggest?

choices.A: Marisol begins to understand the garden as something she can study and make decisions about.
choices.B: Marisol decides that gardening records are useless.
choices.C: Marisol believes the garden should be planted without planning.
choices.D: Marisol wants to stop helping after harvesting one radish.

questions.correctAnswerText: Marisol begins to understand the garden as something she can study and make decisions about.
questions.explanation: The final sentence shows that Marisol sees the garden as something she can learn to interpret.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Last Row.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Marisol reads that radishes had grown well in the row before.
tiles.C: Her father says, “Good choice for that row.”
tiles.D: Marisol plants lettuce in every row without thinking.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding a Mortgage Escrow Statement
A mortgage escrow statement explains how a lender used money collected from a homeowner to pay property taxes and insurance. Many homeowners pay a portion of these costs each month as part of their mortgage payment. The lender holds the money in an escrow account and then pays bills when they are due.
The statement usually lists deposits into the account, payments made from the account, the projected balance, and any shortage or surplus. A shortage can occur if property taxes or insurance premiums rise and the monthly escrow collection was not enough. A surplus can occur if the lender collected more than needed.
Homeowners should review the statement instead of assuming the new payment is wrong. A higher monthly payment may reflect higher taxes, higher insurance premiums, or repayment of an escrow shortage. If the numbers seem unclear, the homeowner can compare the statement with tax bills, insurance notices, and previous mortgage statements.
An escrow statement does not control the amount of property tax or insurance charged. It shows how the lender collected and paid those costs. Understanding the statement can help homeowners plan for changes, ask better questions, and avoid being surprised by a new monthly payment.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A mortgage escrow statement helps homeowners understand tax and insurance payments collected through a mortgage.
choices.B: A mortgage escrow statement decides whether property taxes should exist.
choices.C: Homeowners should ignore escrow shortages and surpluses.
choices.D: A lender uses escrow money only for home repairs.

questions.correctAnswerText: A mortgage escrow statement helps homeowners understand tax and insurance payments collected through a mortgage.
questions.explanation: The passage explains deposits, payments, shortages, surpluses, and payment changes in escrow accounts.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding a Mortgage Escrow Statement.”
What is an escrow account used for in the passage?

choices.A: Holding money collected to pay property taxes and insurance
choices.B: Recording the homeowner’s grocery expenses
choices.C: Setting the value of every house in the neighborhood
choices.D: Replacing the homeowner’s insurance policy

questions.correctAnswerText: Holding money collected to pay property taxes and insurance
questions.explanation: The passage says the lender holds escrow money and pays tax and insurance bills when due.

choices.B: The lender may stop paying tax and insurance bills.
choices.C: The statement may remove all deposits from the account.
choices.D: A surplus always causes monthly payments to rise.

questions.correctAnswerText: Property taxes or insurance premiums may rise, or there may be an escrow shortage.
questions.explanation: These changes can cause the monthly mortgage payment to increase.

choices.B: “The statement usually lists deposits into the account, payments made from the account, the projected balance, and any shortage or surplus.”
choices.C: “An escrow statement does not control the amount of property tax or insurance charged.”
choices.D: “Homeowners should review the statement instead of assuming the new payment is wrong.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “An escrow statement does not control the amount of property tax or insurance charged.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly states that the statement shows activity but does not set the charges.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Use Peer Review Before Major Reports Leave the Firm
A consulting firm should require peer review before major reports are sent to clients. Peer review means that another qualified employee checks the report for accuracy, logic, clarity, and missing information. Consultants often work under deadlines, and even careful writers can overlook errors in charts, assumptions, or wording. A second reader can catch problems before they reach the client.
Peer review would also improve consistency. Clients should not receive reports that vary widely depending on which consultant prepared them. A reviewer can check whether the report follows the firm’s standards for evidence, formatting, and recommendations. This process protects the client and the firm’s reputation.
The requirement does not need to delay every project. The firm could limit mandatory peer review to high-value reports, regulatory submissions, or reports that include complex data. A clear checklist and deadline would keep the process efficient. Spending an extra hour on review is better than sending a report with errors that require an apology or correction later.
Passage B: Keep Peer Review Optional
A consulting firm should keep peer review optional rather than requiring it for major reports. Consultants are already responsible for checking their own work, and managers review many reports before they are sent. Adding another required step could slow projects, especially when clients need quick answers.
Mandatory peer review may also create unclear responsibility. If a report contains an error after review, who is accountable: the writer, reviewer, or manager? Too many checkpoints can make employees assume someone else will catch problems. A stronger approach is to train consultants to use better self-checking methods and to hold project leads responsible for final quality.
Optional peer review is still useful. Consultants should ask for a second reader when a report is unusually complex, when data are uncertain, or when the writer is new to the topic. But requiring review for all major reports may waste time on documents that experienced consultants can handle accurately. The firm should encourage judgment, not create another automatic step.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports required peer review for certain major reports, while Passage B argues peer review should remain optional.
choices.B: Passage A argues that clients should never receive reports, while Passage B argues that reports should contain errors.
choices.C: Passage A supports removing managers from projects, while Passage B supports ending self-checking.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on gardening, while Passage B focuses on mortgage statements.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports required peer review for certain major reports, while Passage B argues peer review should remain optional.
questions.explanation: Passage A supports a requirement for major reports; Passage B supports judgment-based optional review.

choices.B: Managers already review many reports before they are sent.
choices.C: Too many checkpoints can make responsibility unclear.
choices.D: Consultants should ask for help when data are uncertain.

questions.correctAnswerText: A second reader can catch errors in charts, assumptions, or wording before the report reaches the client.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports Passage A’s argument for peer review.

choices.A: Required peer review could slow projects when clients need quick answers.
choices.B: Too many checkpoints could make responsibility unclear.
choices.C: Peer review makes every report less accurate.
choices.D: Consultants never work under deadlines.
choices.E: Managers should stop reviewing reports entirely.

questions.correctAnswerText: Required peer review could slow projects when clients need quick answers. | Too many checkpoints could make responsibility unclear.
questions.explanation: Passage B uses these points to argue against mandatory peer review.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: “Peer review means that another qualified employee checks the report.”

choices.C: “The firm could limit mandatory peer review to high-value reports, regulatory submissions, or reports that include complex data.”
choices.D: “A second reader can catch problems before they reach the client.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “The firm could limit mandatory peer review to high-value reports, regulatory submissions, or reports that include complex data.”
questions.explanation: This sentence addresses delay by limiting the requirement to selected reports.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about peer review.

choices.A: Both authors value accurate reports, but they disagree about whether required peer review is the best way to ensure quality.
choices.B: Both authors believe report accuracy is unimportant.

choices.D: Both authors think consultants should never ask for another reader.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors value accurate reports, but they disagree about whether required peer review is the best way to ensure quality.
questions.explanation: Both passages focus on quality, but they recommend different methods.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about peer review.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data on report error rates, review time, client deadlines, and corrections before and after required peer review
choices.B: A list of office plants near the consulting firm’s lobby
choices.C: A history of report covers used by the firm
choices.D: A survey asking consultants which font they prefer

questions.correctAnswerText: Data on report error rates, review time, client deadlines, and corrections before and after required peer review
questions.explanation: This evidence would help evaluate both benefits and costs of required review.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Stacked beside the copier, Denise reviewed the inventory reports before the meeting.

choices.A: Stacked beside the copier, Denise reviewed the inventory reports before the meeting.
choices.B: Denise reviewed the inventory reports stacked beside the copier before the meeting.
choices.C: Denise, stacked beside the copier, reviewed the inventory reports before the meeting.
choices.D: The copier reviewed Denise’s inventory reports before the meeting.

questions.correctAnswerText: Denise reviewed the inventory reports stacked beside the copier before the meeting.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “inventory reports,” the items stacked beside the copier.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The analyst prepared a chart. The chart compared monthly supply costs.

choices.A: The analyst prepared a chart that compared monthly supply costs.
choices.B: The chart prepared the analyst and compared monthly supply costs.
choices.C: Comparing the analyst, monthly supply costs prepared a chart.
choices.D: The analyst prepared, and monthly supply costs compared the chart.

questions.correctAnswerText: The analyst prepared a chart that compared monthly supply costs.
questions.explanation: This sentence combines the ideas clearly without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The director’s approval was required before the order was placed.
choices.B: The directors approval was required before the order was placed.
choices.C: The directors’ approval’s was required before the order was placed.
choices.D: The director approvals’ was required before the order was placed.

questions.correctAnswerText: The director’s approval was required before the order was placed.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the approval belongs to one director.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Require Clear Total Prices Before Online Checkout
Online retailers should be required to show the full total price before customers begin the final checkout steps. Some websites advertise a low product or ticket price but reveal required fees, handling charges, or service costs only after customers have entered personal information or spent time selecting options. This practice makes comparison shopping harder and can pressure customers to finish a purchase they might not have started if the real price had been clear.
Clear total pricing would support fair competition. A company that includes required fees upfront may appear more expensive than a competitor that hides them until later. Customers deserve to compare actual prices, not partial prices. Showing the full total earlier would reward businesses that are honest from the beginning.
Retailers could still list taxes, shipping choices, or optional add-ons separately when those amounts depend on location or customer preference. The rule should focus on required fees that the seller already knows. If a fee is unavoidable, it should not be treated as a surprise.
This requirement would not tell companies what to charge. It would simply require them to present prices clearly. Customers can make better decisions when the real cost is visible before they invest time and personal information.
Passage B:
Do Not Overregulate Online Price Displays
Lawmakers should be cautious about requiring one exact method for showing online prices. Online purchases vary widely. A hotel room, event ticket, subscription, meal delivery, and furniture order may all involve different taxes, local charges, shipping options, discounts, memberships, or optional services. Requiring a full total too early may be difficult when some costs depend on customer choices.
Retailers also need flexibility to explain prices in ways that fit their products. A website may show a base price first because customers still need to choose size, delivery speed, location, or service level. If the law is written too broadly, businesses may face penalties for normal price changes that occur during a customer’s selections.
There are better ways to protect customers. Regulators can require that fees be disclosed clearly before payment, prohibit misleading advertisements, and enforce rules against fake discounts. Consumer education can also help shoppers look for shipping, service, and subscription charges before agreeing to buy.
Customers need accurate information, but a strict upfront total-price requirement may create confusion for purchases where the final amount cannot be known immediately.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether online retailers should be required to show clear total prices before final checkout steps? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
questions.correctAnswerText: Not auto-scored
questions.explanation: Extended Response answers should be reviewed using the rubric guidance. Strong responses make a clear claim, use evidence from both passages, explain why one argument is better supported, and maintain formal organization and language.
